channel.title: Comments for page "Bigger Than Jesus"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Bigger Than Jesus"

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to the inclusion of NC and ND in the image's license. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're picking up on the subtleties.

Also, I just added a quick reference to SCP-3000 that fits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I'll fix it. I want to fix it."

I just realised. That sounds almost like the influence of 12 on its own. Even though she's not consciously affected by 12, it's still got some hooks in her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had a blast with this one, the 1969-EX in particular was just great. I'm reading through the phone call, genuinely amused by the little tie ins to the real world conspiracy, and what I believe to be a reference to SCP-1901. So distracted by the silliness with the subject of 'Paul Is Dead', that I was blindsided by that final comment:

POI: Right. And for all their music, still, only three died on the Mount that day.

…which sent a goddamn chill down my spine. +1 for lulling me into a false sense of security, before dragging me back into the horror of the overarching mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came up with it off the top of my head. Slightly daft, but sums it up succinctly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to point out that I love that term: Schrödinger's Paul. Excellent description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is meta-textual gold, right there.

Thank you! That's been one of the most ironic aspects of SCP-012 for me. It's about a bunch of people who think they can complete something but cannot, in an article that everyone wants to complete, but cannot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed something while re-reading this. This is just a theory, but it makes sense in context.

"And for all their music, still, only three died on the Mount that day."

If you take that sentence into account, it changes the whole tale changes the focus.

What if Paul didn't die, but Ringo, John and George did? That would explain the confusion over Schrödinger's Paul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a little iffy on this (partly because I was having trouble following the logic around the dead/alive/explained/neutralised stuff, and partly because Emma seems quite a weak character here) but this comment about the 012 file turned it around entirely:

How many people have read that? How many have loved it? Does it deserve that love? Does that document deserve so much attention? And how many want nothing more than to rewrite it? They know how to fix it. They know how to complete it. And yet, they cannot.

That is meta-textual gold, right there. I am a sucker for taking wiki-facts and creating in-universe fiction, and this is a great use of that trope to drive forward the story. Which, in turn, improves the skip … that no-one can resist trying to improve. Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is all way above my paygrade. D: Are these things usually this erudite?

My first and final contribution will be to suggest that the length of the file and/or amount it has been compressed, time-wise, is somehow significant. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the ARG, I can at least get things started:

  • There doesn't appear to be anything hidden in the ID3 tags of the MP3, including "hidden" (PRIV) tags that take extra effort to view,
  • The file is approximately the correct size for its bitrate and length, meaning it's somewhat less likely that there's an additional file "wrapped" inside of it somewhere.
  • The words are to "I Am The Walrus", and appear to be all correct (except for a few times where the "goo goo g'joob"s don't quite match up)

If there's something hidden there, it's not in those places. However, there's a lot of places to hide data in an MP3: between frames (the blocks that make up the MP3), with various encoding tricks, etc.

If anybody who knows more about audio wants to play with this, please go ahead. A few things to try that I can't do on my work computer:

  • Analysis of individual frames,
  • Spectrum analysis,
  • Look for audio bands that aren't normally audible, above or below normal hearing range.

Good luck!

Edit: I'm suspicious that a form of audio steganography was used known as "echo hiding", but I'm not familiar enough with the method to test the theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a mistake. He's referring to Rita, not calling Emma Rita.

ETA: And 043 doesn't talk about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"More popular than Jesus," Roger corrected. Realization passed over his face. "Oh. Oh, Rita. Listen, have you got the Twelve documentation? Show me the full record. I'm still cleared for it, no amnesiacs, remember?"

Roger calls Emma Rita here. This is a strange enough story I'm not entirely sure it is a mistake.

Also I'm surprised SCP-043 didn't come up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I didn't keep track of the conversation very well, but it was pretty thrilling for what it is. That Paul McCartney EX is fun, too. I don't get the ARG thing at all, but I'm sure some smart cookie will figure it out and I can enjoy it in hindsight. This at least stands on its own for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious how they managed to avoid running afoul of the now-former O5-6's vendetta at the time.

Still not sure as to what's going on, it seems to drag a bit in places. Also, I can't see a group like the Foundation legitimatizing Derrida. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I enjoyed reading it… couldn't stop actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. A lot.

The vibe I'm getting is that 012 is memetic in a way that wasn't recognised at first, and also that something is deeply wrong, but no-one is sure what.

I very much also like the implications that whatever the possibly memetic effect is, it's spreading beyond just 012.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yay, you posted it! +1, of course, and happy to help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a tale that desperately wants to be a skip. And not a very good skip either.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I'm ever gonna like any story involving 1841-EX.

More to the point, a lot of this feels like nonsense. The problem is that this story is heavily rooted in reality (at least, this universe's version of reality, IE not dreams). Yet a lot of the logic and the way the story hinges its drama and tension on feel very ungrounded and disorienting. It made it incredibly hard to follow, but more importantly, it felt boring. I can't care about this, or the fact that Paul Is Dead was a thing the Foundation wrote up (especially how contrived it all is in-universe).

It feels like you wanted to take a real-world concept and intertwine it with Foundation lore. And then that gets tied into the 012 reference this storyline hinges on. And I have no problem with 012 being used in tales; I loved the journals. But this rockets the whole concept for me straight into inplausibility. I got everything that went on here, it just feels way too heavy for what this story can carry, and comes off absurd as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More Mount Golgotha stuff, yes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And here we have the second story for On Mount Golgotha.

Picture of Paul McCartney from RV1864 on Flickr.

Had to borrow a tale from Randomini, too. Thank you for reading that, it's perfect.

And many thanks to LadyKatie, Cyantreuse, and thekillerax for review!
